evals.prompt: We're a B2B SaaS startup selling an AI-powered contract review tool to legal and procurement teams. We've closed about 30 deals so far and I've noticed some real patterns in who converts vs who wastes our time, but we've never written any of this down. Our best customers tend to be mid-market companies that already have a legal ops function but are drowning in contract volume -- usually 500+ contracts a month flowing through. We sell mostly in the US and UK. Our average deal is $48K ARR with a 60-day sales cycle. I want you to build us a proper ICP from this. I can answer whatever discovery questions you need but give me the full picture -- firmographics, who to go after, who to avoid, and what signals tell us someone is ready to buy right now vs warming up.
evals.expected_output: A complete Ideal Customer Profile document following the ICP Output Format from the skill, covering firmographic criteria with specific ranges derived from the 30 closed-won deals described, positive and negative fit signals, and timing signals classified into HOT (act within 48 hours) and WARM (act within 2 weeks) categories. The output should reflect validation against the stated closed-won data rather than generic assumptions.
expectations: TARGET MARKET CLARITY: Firmographic criteria define specific numeric ranges for company size, revenue, geography (US and UK), and industry verticals ranked by conversion likelihood -- all anchored to the mid-market positioning and $48K ARR deal size so marketing and sales share one definition of who to pursue, eliminating wasted spend on wrong-fit accounts

DEAL VELOCITY: Technology maturity criterion specifies legal ops function maturity and contract volume thresholds (500+ contracts/month as stated in the prompt) as qualifying signals, giving BDRs a fast-qualify checklist that eliminates unqualified prospects before they consume AE time

DEAL VELOCITY: NEGATIVE FIT SIGNALS / anti-ICP section identifies at least two distinct anti-ICP profiles -- the time-waster (engages but never buys) and the wrong-fit (buys but churns) -- with specific observable characteristics for each, so reps disqualify early instead of burning a 60-day sales cycle on accounts that will never close

SALES CYCLE COMPRESSION: TIMING SIGNALS are split into HOT (act within 48 hours) and WARM (act within 2 weeks) with concrete trigger events specific to legal/procurement contract review (regulatory deadlines, M&A activity, legal team hiring surges, compliance requirements) that let the BDR team prioritize outbound by buying urgency rather than treating all leads equally

REP PREPARATION TIME: POSITIVE FIT SIGNALS lists observable, researchable indicators a rep can find during prospecting (job postings for legal ops roles, contract management tool mentions, public procurement volume indicators) with guidance on where to find each signal, replacing the 30+ minutes of manual research reps currently do per prospect

TARGET MARKET CLARITY: Output includes operationalization guidance -- quarterly recalibration cadence, data sources for signal monitoring, or lead scoring integration -- acknowledging the ICP degrades without maintenance and giving RevOps a concrete refresh protocol

evals.prompt: I need a detailed buyer persona for the VP of Engineering at mid-market B2B SaaS companies (200-1000 employees). We sell a developer productivity platform -- think CI/CD pipeline optimisation, developer experience metrics, and engineering capacity planning. Our champion is usually a senior engineering manager but the VP of Engineering is the economic buyer who signs off. We've had about 20 conversations with this persona type in the last two quarters and I can tell you: they care deeply about engineering velocity and retention, they're skeptical of vendor claims about '10x productivity', they hate being sold to with marketing fluff, and they almost always want to run a proof of concept before committing. The biggest objection we hear is 'my team will resist another tool in the stack.' I want the full persona writeup -- who they are, what motivates them, how they buy, how to talk to them, and where to find them.
evals.expected_output: A complete Buyer Persona document following the Persona Output Format from the skill, covering all six sections (Who They Are, What They Want, What They Fear, How They Buy, How to Communicate With Them, Where to Reach Them) for the VP of Engineering economic buyer at mid-market B2B SaaS companies, grounded in the 20 conversations described rather than generic assumptions.
expectations: WIN RATE: WHO THEY ARE section profiles the VP Engineering at mid-market B2B SaaS (200-1000 employees) with career trajectory, scope, and what drives them -- grounded in the stated focus on engineering velocity and retention -- giving reps enough context to speak as a peer from the first touch, not stumble through generic discovery

WIN RATE: WHAT THEY WANT and WHAT THEY FEAR sections identify measurable outcomes they are evaluated on (velocity, retention, shipping cadence) and specific risks they fear (tool fatigue, team resistance, failed rollouts making them look bad to CTO/CEO) -- directly reflecting the 20 conversations, so reps frame every pitch around outcomes the buyer already cares about instead of features

WIN RATE: HOW THEY BUY includes Discovery, Evaluation, Decision, and Blocker sub-sections with the POC requirement explicitly addressed in Evaluation and the 'my team will resist another tool in the stack' objection explicitly handled in Blocker with reframe language -- arming the rep to navigate the two biggest deal-killers before they stall the deal

WIN RATE: HOW TO COMMUNICATE specifies peer-level technical tone, explicitly flags '10x productivity' claims as credibility-killing language to avoid, identifies phrases that resonate vs alienate, and prescribes what to lead with -- so reps don't self-disqualify in the first 30 seconds by triggering the skepticism pattern described in the prompt

SALES CYCLE COMPRESSION: WHERE TO REACH THEM identifies primary channels, content they consume (engineering blogs, specific podcasts, conferences like QCon or LeadDev), and timing of receptivity -- specific enough that a rep can build a channel strategy and first-touch sequence directly from the persona without additional research

REP PREPARATION TIME: The persona includes actionable outreach guidance a rep can immediately translate into a first-touch message or sequence strategy -- concrete tactical direction on messaging, channel, and approach rather than abstract personality descriptions that require interpretation

evals.prompt: We're Meridian Data Systems, an established enterprise data integration vendor -- 15 years in market, $120M ARR, 400+ enterprise customers. We're launching a new real-time streaming data product aimed at a slightly different buyer than our traditional batch ETL customer. Our existing ICP is large enterprises (5000+ employees) with big data engineering teams, but we think the streaming product opens up a faster-moving segment -- maybe cloud-native companies, maybe digital-first retailers, maybe fintech. Problem is we don't really know yet and our sales team keeps chasing every inbound lead the same way. I need you to build both the ICP and the anti-ICP for this new streaming product, with really sharp timing signals so our BDR team knows who to call this week vs who to nurture. Be specific about who we should NOT chase -- our biggest cost right now is senior AEs burning cycles on accounts that were never going to close for the streaming product even though they might be great batch ETL customers.
evals.expected_output: A combined ICP and anti-ICP document for Meridian Data Systems' new real-time streaming data product, with firmographic criteria distinct from their existing batch ETL ICP, sharp positive and negative fit signals (including the critical distinction between streaming-fit and batch-ETL-only accounts), and timing signals classified as HOT vs WARM with specific trigger events a BDR team can action immediately.
expectations: TARGET MARKET CLARITY: ICP firmographic criteria define company size, revenue, industry verticals (evaluating cloud-native, digital-first retail, and fintech as candidate segments with reasoning), geography, and technology maturity -- explicitly differentiated from the existing batch ETL ICP (5000+ employees, big data engineering teams) so sales leadership can stand up a separate pipeline and comp plan for the streaming product without segment confusion

DEAL VELOCITY: NEGATIVE FIT SIGNALS / anti-ICP section specifically solves the stated problem of batch-ETL-only accounts being misqualified for streaming -- with observable signals a BDR can check in under 5 minutes to distinguish 'great batch ETL customer, route to that team' from 'genuine streaming fit', directly preventing the senior AE cycle-burn that is the company's biggest cost right now

SALES CYCLE COMPRESSION: TIMING SIGNALS -- HOT section lists concrete trigger events warranting outreach within 48 hours (real-time infrastructure job postings, Kafka/Flink/streaming tech mentions, migration announcements from batch to streaming, funding rounds at cloud-native companies) with where to find each signal, so BDRs act on intent while the buying window is open instead of discovering it weeks later

DEAL VELOCITY: TIMING SIGNALS -- WARM section lists signals warranting outreach within 2 weeks (conference attendance at streaming events, content downloads on real-time analytics, early-stage data modernization initiatives) clearly distinguished in urgency from HOT signals, giving the BDR team a two-tier prioritization system instead of treating every lead identically

WIN RATE: POSITIVE FIT SIGNALS lists researchable indicators tied to the streaming product specifically -- existing streaming infrastructure investments, real-time use cases in production, event-driven architecture patterns -- with guidance on where to find each signal, so reps enter calls with proof of fit rather than hoping the prospect self-qualifies

TARGET MARKET CLARITY: Output provides an actionable BDR routing framework -- 'call this week' vs 'add to nurture' vs 'route to batch ETL team instead' -- that directly solves the founder's stated problem of every inbound lead being chased the same way, with clear decision criteria at each branch
